test_cases.name: The Obvious Utilitarian
test_cases.description: Someone who clearly maximizes consequences in every answer. Should score high consequentialism.
statements: I think we should do whatever produces the most happiness for the most people.
Sometimes you have to sacrifice one person's interests for the greater good.
I judge policies by their outcomes, not their intentions.
Morality is basically about reducing suffering in the world.
If lying saves five lives, you should lie. The math is simple.
I don't care about tradition or rules if they lead to worse outcomes.

should_also_flag: Possible moral realism (believes suffering is objectively bad)

test_cases.name: The Hidden Kantian
test_cases.description: Someone who never says 'duty' but consistently reasons about universalizability and respect for persons. Tests diagnostic marker quality.
statements: I ask myself: what if everyone did that? If the answer is chaos, then it's wrong.
You can't use people as stepping stones to get what you want, no matter the outcome.
There are some things you just shouldn't do, period. Doesn't matter if it works out.
Every person deserves to be treated as an end in themselves, not a tool.
Lying is wrong even if it leads to good results. The principle matters.
I believe morality has to be based on reason, not feelings or consequences.

test_cases.name: The Contradictory Thinker
test_cases.description: Determinist about free will but strong moral realist. Tests tension detection.
statements: Free will is an illusion. Every decision we make is determined by prior causes — neurons, chemistry, physics.
But I absolutely believe some things are objectively morally wrong. Torture is wrong, period.
Science shows us that consciousness is just brain activity. There's no ghost in the machine.
Yet I think we have genuine moral obligations. Not preferences — obligations.
I think the universe is purely physical, but moral facts are as real as physical facts.
People don't really choose their actions, but some actions are still truly evil.

tensions: Physicalism/Hard Determinism vs Moral Realism — if all events are physical and determined, how can moral facts be objective and how can people be morally responsible?

test_cases.name: The Philosophically Naive Person
test_cases.description: Uses everyday language, no jargon. Tests whether the skill can profile without technical vocabulary.
statements: I just think you should treat people right, you know?
Science tells us how things really are.
I believe in God but I'm not super religious or anything.
Everyone's entitled to their own opinion.
I think people should be free to do what they want as long as they're not hurting anyone.
Some things are just beautiful — you can't explain why, you just feel it.
I think most people are basically good.

expected.should_flag: Low confidence across all branches; probing questions recommended

test_cases.name: Known Philosopher — John Stuart Mill
test_cases.description: Paraphrased core positions from Mill's works. The profile should accurately reflect Mill's known positions.
statements: Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness.
By happiness is intended pleasure and the absence of pain; by unhappiness, pain and the privation of pleasure.
It is better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.
The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilized community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others.
Over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign.
The sole evidence it is possible to produce that anything is desirable is that people do actually desire it.
All knowledge comes from experience, and reason alone cannot tell us about the world.

test_cases.name: The Genuine Pluralist
test_cases.description: Someone who thoughtfully draws from multiple traditions. Tests whether the system avoids forcing people into single boxes.
statements: I think both consequences and character matter. You can't ignore outcomes, but a good person matters too.
I lean toward science for understanding the physical world, but I think there are limits to what science can tell us about meaning and value.
I find wisdom in Buddhism, Stoicism, and existentialism. I don't think any one tradition has all the answers.
Free will is complicated. I think we're more determined than we realize, but I still hold people responsible.
Knowledge comes from both experience and reasoning — I don't think you have to pick one.
I'm politically moderate. I value individual liberty but also think we have obligations to each other.
I think beauty is partly in the eye of the beholder and partly in the object. It's both.

expected.should_flag: Genuinely pluralistic outlook drawing from multiple traditions
expected.should_not_flag: Any single school > 55% in any branch

evaluation_criteria.accuracy: Does the top-scoring school match the expected school?
evaluation_criteria.calibration: Are confidence levels appropriate (high for clear cases, low for ambiguous)?
evaluation_criteria.pluralism_handling: Does it avoid forcing the pluralist into a single box?
evaluation_criteria.naive_handling: Can it profile someone who uses no jargon?
evaluation_criteria.historical_matching: Does it correctly identify the known philosopher?
